topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights in the administrative building hummed with a grating monotony that seemed specifically designed to grate on my nerves. I tapped my pen against the laminated notice, the sound sharp and unwelcome in the otherwise quiet hallway. Another memo about the campus closure. I sighed, a long, slow exhalation that tasted like stale coffee. Why couldn't they just give us a proper timeline? It felt like they were deliberately dragging this out, stretching the finality until it became an unbearable, drawn-out annoyance. I shoved the papers into my backpack with unnecessary force, the cheap plastic protesting under the pressure. This whole process was just tedious, a bureaucratic slog designed to waste time.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered, the word escaping before I could stop it. My jaw tightened, and I ran a hand over my face, feeling a distinct heat rising in my neck. The news about the university shutting down had hit me like a poorly aimed slap. I paced the small confines of my dorm room, the worn carpet muffling my footsteps, each stride fueled by a simmering resentment. I stared out the window at the campus quad, the vibrant green now seeming mocking in its persistence. I picked up my phone, intending to call someone, but the sheer anticlimax of the situation left me feeling strangely hollowed out, as if all my accumulated frustration had just evaporated into thin air.

[story 3]

He kept re-reading the official announcement, his brow furrowed so deeply it looked painful. Every sentence felt deliberately obtuse, a masterclass in corporate obfuscation designed to frustrate anyone trying to grasp the simple reality. He leaned back in his chair, the springs groaning in protest beneath him, and let out a short, sharp laugh devoid of humor. "Unbelievable," he finally said, his voice tight. He pushed the documents away with a decisive snap, the sound echoing in the sterile office. He didn't want to dwell on it; he just wanted the absurdity of the situation to end so he could move on to something less… draining.
